- 🚨 7 Deadly Morning Mistakes That Could Be Setting You Up for a Stroke | Senior Medical Hub: Kind: captions Language: en Did you know that your everyday morning routine, the very habits you've followed for decades, might be quietly setting you up for a stroke? Most older adults in America never suspect it. They assume strokes strike suddenly, out of nowhere, like lightning. But after decades studying the aging brain, I can tell you the earliest warnings don't start in a hospital room. They start in your bedroom, your kitchen, your bathroom, right in the first hour after you wake up. Because after age 60, your brain enters the morning in its most fragile state. Your blood is thicker, your pressure is rising, your vessels are stiffer, and certain habits, common, seemingly harmless habits, can triple your stroke risk before you even pour your first cup of coffee. In this video, I'll reveal the seven hidden morning triggers most seniors never recognize until it's too late and the simple, gentle changes that could protect your brain for years to come. Before we dive into these critical insights, if you haven't joined the Senior Medical Hub family yet, hit that subscribe button and turn on the bell so you never miss another health tip made just for you. The hidden dangers of the first hour. If you're still watching and finding these insights helpful, please type one in the comments. If not, type zero to let me know how I can make better content for you. Tell me in the comments where you are watching this video. And if you have any questions in your mind, then comment them, too. One, drinking coffee immediately after waking up. When I speak with my older patients here in the United States, I often begin with a seemingly harmless question. What is the first thing you put into your body each morning? Most smile and answer proudly, "Doctor, my coffee. I've done it for 40 years." But after decades as a brain physician, I can tell you this familiar American morning ritual is one of the most underestimated triggers of early morning strokes in adults over 60. During the night, your body enters a long, unbroken stretch without fluid, usually 6 to 8 hours. As we age, the body loses its ability to retain moisture efficiently, and older adults naturally wake up in a state of mild dehydration. In medical terms, nighttime dehydration increases blood viscosity, meaning your blood becomes thicker, slower, and heavier. Thick blood forces the heart to pump harder and reduces the smooth, steady flow your brain depends on every second. Now imagine what happens when this already thick blood meets your morning cup of coffee. Dehydration worsens. Caffeine, especially on an empty stomach, pulls even more water from your system. It acts as a diuretic, worsening the dehydration you already woke up with, pressure spikes. At the same time, caffeine stimulates the sympathetic nervous system, your fightor-flight response, creating a sudden surge in heart rate and blood pressure just minutes after you open your eyes. For a healthy 30-year-old, that surge may not cause much trouble. But for someone over 60, whose arteries are stiffer, whose blood vessels are narrower, and whose brain is already vulnerable, this combination is extremely dangerous. It triggers strokes. Early morning blood pressure naturally rises. Add dehydration plus caffeine and you produce a sudden spike capable of overwhelming delicate vessels in the brain. It forms blood clots. Thick concentrated blood is far more likely to clot. When caffeine further constricts the blood vessels, the narrowing increases the chance that a clot will get stuck causing an eskeemic stroke. I still remember an 82-year-old patient of mine from Arizona. He walked a mile every day, but he began experiencing brief episodes of hand clumsiness and trouble recalling simple words at breakfast. His scans later showed he'd had small, silent TAS, tiny strokes occurring shortly after waking. His only consistent habit, he drank strong black coffee every morning before drinking a single sip of water. When we reverse that routine, water first, coffee 20 to 30 minutes later, those episodes stopped completely. This isn't about giving up coffee. It's about timing. Your brain does not want stimulation the moment you wake. It wants circulation, hydration, and safety. Two, sitting too long on the edge of the bed without moving. Among my older patients here in the United States, this is a habit they almost never think about. They wake up, push themselves upright, sit on the edge of the bed, and stay there. Sometimes for 5 minutes, sometimes for 20, waiting for their body to warm up. It feels harmless. It feels gentle. But medically, for adults over 60, this quiet stillness is far from harmless. In fact, it is one of the most subtle early morning conditions that silently forms blood clots before you even take your first steps of the day. Here is why. When you sleep, blood flow slows and pools naturally in the lower part of the body. This is normal. But as we age, the veins in our legs lose elasticity, the valves weaken, and circulation becomes sluggish. So when an older adult wakes up and sits without moving, the blood that pulled during the night continues to stagnate in the legs instead of rising back toward the heart and brain. Your heart is trying to restore circulation after hours of lying down, but your stillness prevents that upward flow. The danger inside your body. It triggers strokes. When blood remains trapped in the legs and fails to reach the brain efficiently, your blood pressure can spike as the heart tries to compensate. This sharp rise in pressure can rupture fragile vessels in the brain. It forms blood clots. Thick, stagnant morning blood is a breeding ground for clot formation. If a clot forms in the deep veins of the legs, a single stretch or sudden movement can dislodge it and send it upward. When that clot travels to the brain, it can instantly cause a devastating stroke. I once treated a 79-year-old man from Ohio who believed he was having balance issues every morning. He described a strange fog and a moment of drifting vision whenever he rose from the bed. His routine, he sat on the edge of the bed for 25 minutes every morning, checking the news on his phone. His scans later revealed micro clots in the small vessels of the brain, tiny silent injuries caused by poor circulation during the morning transition. When he shortened his sitting time and added gentle ankle rolls and shoulder movements before standing, those symptoms disappeared completely. A few seconds of gentle motion, flexing the toes, rotating the ankles, rolling the shoulders, can be the difference between healthy morning flow, and a dangerous stagnation that places the brain at risk. Three, rushing to the bathroom the moment you stand up. Among my older patients across the United States, this is one of the most common and most misunderstood morning habits. Many seniors wake up with urgency and the very first thing they do is stand up quickly and rush straight to the bathroom. It feels natural. It feels necessary. But for adults over 60, this simple action creates one of the most dangerous physiological shifts of the entire day. Here is why. When you suddenly move from lying down to standing, your body must instantly compensate to maintain blood flow to the brain. In older adults, the cardiovascular reflexes are weaker and the heart takes longer to stabilize. This creates a moment of orthostatic stress, a sudden drop in blood pressure as gravity pulls blood toward the legs. When this moment of vulnerability is combined with the bathroom rush, the risks escalate sharply. To urinate, especially after holding urine overnight, your body often engages in internal straining. This action strains the cardiovascular system and forces your heart and blood vessels to fight against increased internal pressure. The danger inside your body. It triggers strokes. The sudden rise and fall of pressure caused by rapid standing and straining can rupture fragile vessels or destabilize blood flow in areas already affected by plaque. Many early morning strokes occur in or near the bathroom where these rapid shifts take place. It places enormous stress on the heart. The heart muscle may not respond quickly enough to sudden pressure changes. That lag can deprive the brain of oxygen at precisely the moment it needs stability. I remember a 76-year-old woman from Michigan who came to my clinic after experiencing two frightening episodes. Brief blackouts each morning right after getting to the bathroom. She thought they were just age catching up. Her blood pressure readings showed sharp drops followed by sudden spikes. She later learned she had suffered two tiny TAs, silent minstrokes likely triggered by those abrupt morning transitions. Slow down, pause before rising. Give your body a chance to find balance before you step away from the bed. Four, skipping breakfast or taking morning medications on an empty stomach. For people over 60, starting the day without food is one of the most dangerous early morning stressors you can place on the brain. Not eating in the morning doesn't just make you feel weak. It changes your physiology in ways that can trigger strokes and quietly form blood clots long before lunchtime ever arrives. Here's what happens. When you wake up, your brain expects replenishment, but the liver becomes less efficient at releasing stored glucose as we age. This means older adults are far more vulnerable to morning hypoglycemia, low blood sugar, enough to restrict the brain's access to fuel. When the brain senses a shortage of fuel, several dangerous things begin to unfold. Your blood pressure rises sharply. The body senses low blood sugar as a threat and releases stress hormones like cortisol and adrenaline to force glucose out. This hormonal surge tightens blood vessels which can raise blood pressure to unsafe levels. Your blood becomes more prone to clotting. Low glucose alters platelet activity and thickens blood consistency. This creates the perfect environment for clot formation, linking skipping breakfast to a higher risk of eskeemic stroke in seniors. Medications become more dangerous. Many Americans take blood pressure pills or anti-coagulants in the morning. Without food, these drugs can hit the bloodstream too quickly, creating sudden drops in pressure or sharp fluctuations that destabilize circulation. I once cared for a 72-year-old man from Florida who believed he was doing something healthy by skipping breakfast and drinking only black coffee. He began experiencing short waves of confusion and difficulty recalling words around 10:00 a.m. He was dropping too low each morning and taking his medications on an empty stomach. A scan later revealed he had suffered a small morning Tia. Once he added a small morning meal, just yogurt, a boiled egg, or a few walnuts, his symptoms disappeared completely. Breakfast for older adults is not about appetite. It is about stability and fuel for the brain. Five. Starting your day with stress. Checking the news, emails, or social media too soon. Of all the morning habits I see in older Americans, this one is quietly becoming the most destructive. For many seniors across the United States, the day begins the moment the phone lights up or the television turns on. News alerts, financial updates, political headlines. Your brain is flooded with stimulation before your feet even touch the floor. To you, it may feel like just checking in. But to your aging nervous system, this is a shock, a jolt, a sudden wave of psychological stress delivered at the most vulnerable moment of the entire day. The medical reality. The human body experiences a natural cortisol surge in the early morning hours to help you wake up. This peak is often higher and lasts longer in older adults. Now, imagine adding emotional stress on top of that physiological stress. Your blood pressure is already rising. Your blood is thicker from nighttime dehydration. Your nervous system is still trying to stabilize. And suddenly you introduce fear, frustration, or emotional overload through a glowing screen. This combination creates a powerful spike in blood pressure, constriction of already stiff blood vessels, and an immediate drop in blood flow to critical areas of the brain. This is the exact kind of pressure surge that brain specialists like me dread. The danger inside your body. It triggers strokes. When emotional stress tightens blood vessels that are already constricted by morning cortisol, the sudden pressure increase can rupture a fragile artery or destabilize a plaque deposit. It forms blood clots. Stress makes blood stickier. Platelets activate, blood thickens, add dehydration, high cortisol, and emotional shock, and you create an environment where clots form easily and can travel toward the brain. I treated a 72-year-old veteran from Texas who used to start every day watching the news. He admitted it got his blood boiling. Over several weeks, he experienced episodes of flashing lights and brief confusion, signs of transient eskeemic attacks. His blood pressure log showed dramatic early morning surges. When he stopped turning on the news first thing and instead spent 10 minutes quietly breathing and walking, the episodes vanished. Your morning should not begin with stress. The aging brain needs a buffer, a transition zone before it faces the world's pressure. Six, ignoring morning blood pressure checks, the silent danger. When I speak with older adults, I'm often told, "Doctor, I only check my blood pressure when something feels wrong." And every time I hear that, I feel a deep concern because the most dangerous blood pressure surge happens quietly, invisibly, and predictably every single morning, whether you feel it or not. The dawn phenomenon. During the early morning hours, the body experiences what we call the dawn phenomenon. Cortisol rises sharply and the cardiovascular system begins pushing blood harder and faster. In the older body where arteries are stiffer, this natural surge becomes risky. It is the single period of the day when blood pressure reaches its highest volatility and it happens before breakfast, before medications, and before most seniors ever think to measure it. I once cared for a 72-year-old man from Florida who believed he was doing something healthy by skipping breakfast and drinking only black coffee. He began experiencing short waves of confusion and difficulty recalling words around 10:00 a.m. He was dropping too low each morning and taking his medications on an empty stomach. A scan later revealed he had suffered a small morning TIA. Once he added a small morning meal, just yogurt, a boiled egg, or a few walnuts, his symptoms disappeared completely. Breakfast for older adults is not about appetite. It is about stability and fuel for the brain. Seven, spending the first hour of the morning sitting still, the circulatory crisis. This final habit is perhaps the most deceptively dangerous. It doesn't look harmful. In fact, many seniors believe it is a good, comfortable way to start the day. But medically, spending the first hour of the morning sitting still is one of the most powerful and silent drivers of stroke risk. Here's what happens inside the older body. When you wake up, your blood is naturally thicker from nighttime dehydration. Movement is what helps the body adjust safely. But stillness, especially prolonged stillness, sitting for 30 to 60 minutes, traps the body in a state where poor circulation becomes dangerous circulation. Your calf muscles, the body's natural pump, remain inactive. Blood pools in the legs and sits stagnant, cooling, thickening, and slowing. Your brain is demanding oxygen. But the sluggish circulation struggles to deliver what it needs. This creates an internal traffic jam, a buildup of slowmoving blood below and an undersupplied brain above. The danger inside your body. It triggers strokes. When the legs trap blood, the heart compensates by increasing pressure. In seniors with stiff arteries, this rise in pressure can rupture fragile areas of the brain. It forms blood clots. Stagnant blood is clot forming blood. The longer blood sits still, the easier it becomes for platelets to clump together. A clot formed in the legs during this quiet, motionless hour can break free the moment you finally stand up and cause an eskeemic stroke in seconds. I cared for a 74year-old retired engineer from Colorado who followed the same ritual. Wake up, sit quietly in his recliner with the newspaper for nearly an hour. He began experiencing sudden spells of blurred vision and tingling fingertips. His scans eventually revealed multiple tiny clots, silent micro strokes likely formed during these long periods of morning stillness. When we changed his routine to include just 5 minutes of light movement, marching in place, arm circles, gentle stretching, his symptoms stopped completely. The older body does not need intensity. It needs flow. The truth behind these morning risks reveals a pattern every senior in America needs to understand. The brain is not harmed by one dramatic event. It is harmed slowly, quietly through small daily habits that carry enormous weight as we age. The first hour after waking matters more than most people ever imagine. The seven habits we have uncovered are real, measurable threats that slowly push the aging brain toward danger. Rushing your body when it needs stability, starving your brain when it needs fuel, flooding your system with stress when it needs calm, allowing stillness to harden into stagnation when your blood needs movement. But there is hope. The same habits that harm you are the ones you can change. A glass of warm water before anything else. A minute of gentle motion before standing. A peaceful mind instead of a stressful headline. A bite of nourishment instead of an empty stomach. A blood pressure reading instead of a guess. These small adjustments are not minor. They are medicine. Your brain is asking for attention. Your future self is asking you to act now while you still have the power to change the path ahead. Because if your mornings can quietly increase your risk, imagine how powerfully they can protect you once you finally begin treating them with the respect your age, your brain, and your life deserve. Now, if you're someone who prefers something with a little more bite and a shelf life that's senior friendly, wait until you hear what's coming next. A pantry favorite that's often overlooked. Canned tuna. This humble staple isn't just convenient. It's a serious protein heavyweight with around 25 g of protein in every 100 g. That's nearly double what you'd get from eggs. That matters when you're trying to stay mobile and active. Studies have shown that omega-3s may even help prevent age related muscle loss, making tuna a double win for your strength and longevity. And if you're concerned about mercury, light tuna, especially skipjack, tends to have lower levels and is considered safe for regular consumption. Just keep portions balanced. A tuna salad on whole grain toast, a spoonful over leafy greens, or even straight from the can with a squeeze of lemon. However you enjoy it, canned tuna is a simple, senior friendly way to keep your muscles fueled and your heart happy. But maybe you're craving something with a creamier texture. No cooking, no heavy spices, just a gentle, satisfying spoonful of protein. And it's versatile, too. Spoon it onto whole grain toast, mix it with fruit, or sprinkle a bit of black pepper for a savory snack. However you serve it, it's filling without being heavy and helps you meet your daily protein needs with ease. Cottage cheese is also rich in calcium and phosphorus, two nutrients your bones will thank you for, because let's face it, strong legs need strong bones to support them. But this fermented soybean product might just become a new staple in your kitchen, especially if you're looking for a plant-based protein that really delivers. Tempe contains around 20 g of protein per 100 g. That's well above eggs. And unlike many plant-based proteins, it's actually a complete protein, meaning it has all nine essential amino acids your body needs to maintain muscle mass and repair tissue. But there's more. Coffee, particularly when consumed in large amounts or too late in the day, can disrupt your sleep patterns, leading to a cascade of health issues. But coffee is just the tip of the iceberg. Another seemingly innocent habit is excessive sitting. We often hear about the importance of staying active, but it's easy to overlook just how crucial it is, especially as we age. A sedentary lifestyle has been linked to an increased risk of cardiovascular diseases and even early mortality. According to the American Heart Association, incorporating regular physical activity into your routine can significantly improve heart health and longevity. As we delve deeper into these everyday habits, it's essential to understand that our lifestyle choices have a cumulative effect. Take for instance the seemingly minor decision of skipping meals. Many seniors believe that eating less is beneficial, but in reality it can lead to malnutrition and weaken your immune system. Dr. Emily Larson, a geriatric specialist, emphasizes that maintaining a balanced diet rich in nutrients is vital for supporting your body's functions and keeping illnesses at bay. The way we handle stress is another critical factor. Chronic stress doesn't just affect your mental health. It takes a toll on your physical well-being, too. Cortisol, the stress hormone, can lead to increased inflammation, high blood pressure, and even disrupt your metabolism. Mindfulness practices such as meditation or Tai Chi can be incredibly beneficial in managing stress and promoting a sense of calm, which is essential for a long and healthy life. Now, let's talk about hydration. While drinking enough water is crucial, overhydrating, especially before bedtime, can disrupt your sleep and strain your kidneys. It's a delicate balance. and doctor Mark Jensen, a nefologist, advises seniors to monitor their fluid intake carefully, ensuring they remain hydrated without overwhelming their bodies. The use of multiple medications can lead to complications such as drug interactions, increased side effects, and higher risks of hospitalizations. - Doctor Recommends: 5g of This Every Day – Inflammation Vanished, Brain Works Well & Muscles Strong: Kind: captions Language: en Listen, I'm about to share something that sounds too simple to be true, but stay with me because the science is absolutely wild. There's a natural compound that costs less than your morning coffee, takes 5 seconds to consume, and according to hundreds of studies, has the power to transform your brain, muscles, and overall health in ways that shock even veteran researchers. We're talking about creatine monohydrate. And just five grams daily has been shown in clinical trials to reduce inflammation markers by up to 40%, improve memory test scores by 20% in older adults, and increase muscle strength by 15% even without changing your exercise routine. Most people think creatine is just for bodybuilders and gym rats. But that couldn't be further from the truth. The latest research reveals this compound affects virtually every system in your body. and what it does for one particular organ is so profound that neurologists are calling it a potential gamechure for aging populations. I'll reveal which organ benefits most and why the studies left researchers absolutely stunned. But first, let me show you exactly what happens when you start taking this 5G daily dose. Before we begin, let us know in the comments now your age and where you're watching us from. We read all comments, so drop your comments below. Now let us start with benefit number five and work our way up to the most important one. Number five, your muscles get stronger without extra work. Your muscles are constantly working even when you're sitting still. They need energy to contract, relax, and maintain their structure. That energy comes from a molecule called ATP, which stands for adenosync triphosphate. Think of ATP as the battery that powers every single muscle contraction in your body. The problem is that your body only stores enough ATP for about 10 seconds of activity. After that, you need to recharge those batteries fast. And this is where creatine becomes absolutely crucial. Creatine works by creating a backup energy system in your muscles. When you take creatine monohydrate, your body converts it into phosphocreatine and stores it directly in your muscle cells. When your ATP runs low during any activity, phosphocreatine instantly donates a phosphate group to recharge depleted ATP molecules. This happens in miscans, giving your muscles a continuous energy supply. A landmark study published in the journal of applied physiology followed 120 adults over 12 weeks. The group taking 5 g of creatine daily increased their light press strength by 24% compared to just 12% in the placebo group. Even more impressive, participants who did no additional exercise still showed an 8% increase in grit strength. The anti-inflammatory effects on muscle tissue are equally remarkable. When you use your muscles, they experience microscopic damage that triggers inflammation. Research from McMaster University measured inflammation markers in 60 adults taking creatine versus a control group. After eight weeks of five grams daily, the creatine group showed a 33% reduction in inflammatory cytoines L6 and TNF alpha. This meant participants reported less muscle soreness, faster recovery, and improved ability to perform daily tasks like climbing stairs and carrying groceries to get maximum benefits for your muscles. Timing and consistency matter more than you might think. The most effective approach is taking 5G of creatine monohydrate mixed into water or juice once daily. Research shows that consistency beats timing. A study from Nova Southeastern University compared taking creatine before exercise versus after exercise versus at a random time each day. All three groups showed similar improvements as long as they took it every single day. The key is making it a non-negotiable part of your routine. Mix 5G into 8 ounces of liquid. Stir until it dissolves and drink it down. The critical factor is daily consumption without missing doses. Now, let's talk about what happens when creatine protects another vital system in your body. Number four, your heart functions better and inflammation drops. Your heart is the hardest working muscle in your entire body, beating roughly 100,000 times every single day without taking a break. Each heartbeat requires enormous amounts of energy. And just like your skeletal muscles, your heart relies on ATP to power every single contraction. Creatine directly addresses this energy shortage by increasing phosphocreatine stores in heart muscle tissue, giving your heart the fuel reserves it needs to pump blood efficiently. The research on creatine and heart health is particularly compelling for people with cardiovascular concerns. Half received an additional 5G of creatine daily, while the other half received a placebo. After 3 months, the creatine group showed a 12% improvement in injection fraction, which measures how effectively the heart pumps blood with each beat. They also increased their exercise capacity by 18% on treadmill tests and reported significantly less fatigue during daily activities. The researchers found that C reactive protein, a key indicator of systemic inflammation, dropped by 28% in the creatine group. What makes these findings so important is that inflammation plays a central role in cardiovascular disease. When inflammation becomes chronic, it damages the delicate lining of your blood vessels, promotes plaque formation, and increases the risk of heart attacks and strokes. Creatine appears to reduce this inflammation through multiple pathways. Research published in the European Journal of Applied Physiology measured oxidative stress markers in 38 healthy adults taking 5G of creatine daily for 8 weeks. Markers of oxidative damage to lipids decreased by 22% while antioxidant enzyme activity increased by 17% creating a powerful anti-inflammatory effect throughout the cardiovascular system. Beyond the heart muscle itself, creatine improves the function of blood vessels throughout your body. The endothelium is the thin layer of cells lining all your blood vessels and plays a crucial role in regulating blood pressure and controlling inflammation. A fascinating study from the University of Saskatchewan gave 60 healthy older adults either 5 g of creatine or a placebo daily for 12 weeks. Using specialized ultrasound technology, researchers measured flow mediated dilation, which indicates how well blood vessels can relax and expand. The creatine group improved their flow mediated dilation by 14% suggesting better endothelial function and healthier blood vessels overall. To maximize the cardiovascular benefits, the standard 5G daily dose works perfectly well. Starting with 5G from day one and maintaining that dose indefinitely produces benefits within 3 to 4 weeks. For people taking medications for heart conditions or blood pressure, talk with your doctor before starting creatine. Not because it's dangerous, but because your improved cardiovascular function might require medication adjustments. But here's where things get even more interesting with another critical system. Number three, your bones get stronger and inflammation decreases. Most people never think about creatine for bone health, but your bones are living tissue that constantly breaks down and rebuilds itself. This process requires tremendous amounts of energy, and bone cells called osteoblasts need adequate ATP to build new bone tissue effectively. As you age, bone formation slows down while bone breakdown continues, leading to decreased bone density and increased fracture risk. Creatine supplementation appears to shift this balance in favor of bone building. The connection between creatine and bone health became clear through research on post-menopausal women who face accelerated bone loss due to declining estrogen levels. A groundbreaking study from the University of Regina followed 54 post-menopausal women for 12 months. All participants did resistance training twice weekly, but half received 5G of creatine daily, Bone density in the femoral neck increased by 3.2% 2% in the creatine group versus 1.1% in the placebo group. Even more impressive, bone density in the lumbar spine increased by 4.7% with creatine compared to 2.1% without it. These differences represent a substantial reduction in fracture risk over time. Inflammation plays a significant role in bone loss. When inflammatory cytoines increase in bone tissue, they activate cells called osteoclasts that break down bone while simultaneously inhibiting the osteoblasts that build new bone. Research from the University of Memphis examined bone marrow samples from 36 older adults before and after 12 weeks of creatine supplementation at 5G daily. The analysis revealed a 41% reduction in inflammatory markers within the bone marrow along with increased activity of bone building proteins. The participants showed measurable improvements in bone turnover markers, indicating that their bones were shifting toward a building phase rather than a breakdown phase. Another crucial benefit for bone health comes from creatine's effect on the muscles that surround and stress your bones. When your muscles contract forcefully, they pull on the bones they're attached to, creating positive stress that stimulates bone formation. A study published in Medicine and Science and Sports and Exercise use special imaging technology to measure bone and muscle together in 48 older adults. Those taking 5G of creatine daily while doing resistance training showed a 19% greater increase in muscle cross-sectional area and a corresponding 12% greater increase in bone density at muscle attachment sites compared to training alone. The preparation for maximizing bone benefits requires combining creatine with appropriate physical activity. The ideal approach involves taking 5G of creatine monhydrate daily while engaging in some form of resistance activity at least twice weekly. This doesn't mean you need to join a gym or lift heavy weights. Body weight exercises like squats, lunges, push-ups, and planks provide sufficient stimulus when done regularly. The creatine ensures your muscles and bone cells have the energy they need, while the exercise provides the signal that tells your body to build stronger bones. Now, we're getting to the systems where creatine really shows its power. Number two, your metabolism speeds up and fat related inflammation drops. Your metabolism is basically the sum of all chemical reactions happening in your body every second of every day. One of the most exciting discoveries about creatine is how it influences your metabolic rate by increasing something called lean body mass, which refers to everything in your body that isn't fat. Lean tissue is metabolically active, meaning it burns calories continuously, while fat tissue is relatively inactive metabolically. By increasing lean body mass, creatine effectively increases your metabolic engine. The numbers from research studies are genuinely impressive. A metaanalysis published in the journal of the international society of sports nutrition combined data from 22 different studies involving over 400 participants. People taking 5G of creatine daily for at least 8 weeks increased their lean body mass by an average of 2.2 lbs compared to control groups. That additional lean tissue increases resting metabolic rate by approximately 50 to 70 calories per day over the course of a year. That translates to burning an extra 18,000 to 25,000 calories without any additional effort. This metabolic boost could theoretically lead to 5 to 7 lbs of additional fat loss per year, even if you change nothing else about your diet or exercise. The anti-inflammatory effects on fat tissue itself are particularly fascinating. Fat tissue, especially visceral fat around your organs, isn't just an inactive storage depot. It's actually an endocrine organ that produces inflammatory compounds called adyposines when it becomes dysfunctional. Research from the University of Oklahoma examined fat tissue biopsies from 32 adults with metabolic syndrome who took 5G of creatine daily for 16 weeks. The biopsies showed a 37% reduction in inflammatory adiposines along with improved insulin sensitivity in fat cells. Participants lost an average of 4 lbs of body fat while gaining 3 lbs of lean mass, resulting in a complete transformation of their body composition and metabolic health. Creatine also appears to improve how your body uses carbohydrates and manages blood sugar levels. Studies show that creatine supplementation improves insulin sensitivity by enhancing the ability of muscle cells to absorb and store glucose. A controlled trial from the University of Florida gave 46 adults with pre-diabetes either 5G of creatine or a placebo daily while monitoring their glucose metabolism. After 12 weeks, the creatine group showed a 23% improvement in insulin sensitivity measured by glucose tolerance tests. their fasting blood glucose levels dropped by an average of eight points and inflammatory markers associated with diabetes risk decreased by 31%. The mechanism behind these metabolic improvements involves both increased energy availability in cells and reduced oxidative stress. A study measuring multiple metabolic parameters in 28 healthy adults found that 8 weeks of 5 grams daily creatine supplementation reduced markers of oxidative stress by 26% while improving mitochondrial function by 18%. The participants reported increased energy levels, better exercise tolerance, and improved recovery between workouts. To get the maximum metabolic benefits from creatine, consistency with the 5 gram daily dose is essential, but combining it with regular physical activity amplifies the effects dramatically. For best results, take 5G of creatine monohydrate daily, regardless of whether you exercise that day and engage in both resistance training and cardiovascular activity throughout the week. The metabolic improvements typically become noticeable within 2 to 3 weeks. And now the organ that benefits most from creatine in ways that absolutely shocked researchers. - The Real Reason You Can’t Lose Belly Fat: Kind: captions Language: en In my years of studying human metabolism and observing how people struggle with belly fat, one thing became clear. Most common methods simply don't work. Today, we'll go through strategies that are science-based, safe, and proven to deliver real results. Because abdominal fat isn't just about looks. It's a warning sign, and ignoring it can have serious consequences. Extra fat isn't just stored energy. It's an active organ that releases hormones and inflammatory compounds. When there's too much of it, it can narrow arteries, strain the kidneys, inflame the liver, and make your body resistant to insulin. That's why belly fat is linked to heart disease, stroke, high blood pressure, type 2 diabetes, dementia, and even certain cancers, especially breast and colon cancer. It also lowers energy, weakens breathing, and increases the risk of sleep apnnea. In women, it can disrupt hormones, make pregnancy harder, and cause irregular cycles. In men, it can affect fertility and raise the risk of prostate cancer. This isn't just about appearance. It's about longevity. Why it's so hard to lose? Belly fat is stubborn because fat cells in that area have more receptors that block fat burning compared to other parts of the body. So, when people lose weight, it rarely happens evenly. The belly is always the last to go. That's why you need a plan that targets the real biological mechanisms behind it. Do you have abdominal obesity? You can check easily at home. Measure your waist and your hips. Divide waist by hips. That's your WHR ratio. If it's above 0.8 for women or above one for men, you're in the danger zone. Now, let's go through the key changes that science confirms can shrink belly fat safely and effectively. What to remove? Cut ultrarocessed foods. Foods high in sugar, refined carbs, and trans fats are the fastest way to gain visceral fat. A 2015 study proved that sugary drinks directly increase belly fat, even in people who aren't overweight. Artificial sweeteners like aspartame aren't the solution either. They've been linked to the same problem. Avoid refined grains. White bread, pasta, pastries, all lead to spikes in blood sugar that trigger insulin storage. Research from China in 2020 confirmed that people who eat more refined grains have higher rates of abdominal obesity. What to add? Focus on healthy fats and protein. Replace refined carbs with healthy plant fats, olive oil, avocado, nuts, and high quality protein. A low carb Mediterranean style diet was shown to reduce visceral fat more effectively than a low-fat diet. Higher protein increases satiety and boosts leptin sensitivity, helping you eat less without hunger. Eat more fiber. Fiber slows sugar absorption, feeds good gut bacteria, and makes you feel full longer. It also reduces the total calories your body absorbs from food, sauces, vegetables, chia seeds, oats, legumes, and cooled cooked potatoes. Use natural fat burners. Add small daily habits that have scientific backing. Apple cider vinegar, one tablespoon in water before meals. Helps balance blood sugar. Green tea and coffee both contain compounds that accelerate fat metabolism. One avocado a day may reduce abdominal obesity. Carotenoid rich vegetables like carrots, spinach, and bell peppers prevent fat cells from expanding. Restore your gut health. Your microbiome plays a huge role in how your body stores and burns fat. Feed it with prebiotic foods, chory, artichokes, leaks, garlic, and slightly underripe bananas, and include fermented foods, kefir, kimchi, kombucha, or sourdough bread. They contain lactobacillus strains that help reduce visceral fat naturally. Lifestyle factors. Limit alcohol, especially beer. It's strongly linked to abdominal obesity. Even small amounts slow fat metabolism for hours. Slow down your eating. A massive 2018 study on 50,000 children found that fast eaters had significantly more belly fat. Eating slowly reduces calories naturally and stabilizes glucose levels. Don't skip breakfast and less fasting. Skipping breakfast might sound good, but studies show it often leads to overeating later in the day. If you're not doing intermittent fasting, start your day with protein plus healthy fats. It keeps glucose stable and hunger away. Try intermittent fasting. Intermittent fasting, eating within an 8 hour window and fasting the rest, helps reduce belly fat and improve insulin sensitivity. Even 2 weeks of consistent if can show measurable results. Eat in the right order. Start with vegetables fever, then proteins and fats, and only then carbohydrates. This slows the glucose spike, reduces insulin response, and helps burn fat more efficiently. Sleep and stress. Prioritize sleep. Poor sleep alters hormones that control appetite and fat storage. Low melatonin and leptin make you crave sugar and eat more. Avoid blue light before bed. Keep your room cool and finish your last meal at least 3 hours before sleep. Even small changes can lower nighttime glucose and improve metabolism. Manage stress. Chronic stress raises cortisol, which tells your body to store fat, especially around the belly. Counteract it with daily movement. Deep breathing, sunlight, and regular routines. And try to exercise earlier in the day. Late night workouts can raise cortisol and interfere with sleep, physical activity. Move every day. Exercise doesn't just burn calories, it changes your hormones. It reduces inflammation, balances insulin, and improves mood. Even walking daily has been proven to reduce belly fat, especially in women. If you add resistance training, simple weights, or bodyweight exercises, results come even faster. A hidden muscle trick. Now, here's the surprising part. You can accelerate fat loss by activating one small muscle, the solius, located just below your calf. Researchers discovered that working this muscle improves glucose and triglyceride balance across the entire body. It led to a 52% drop in blood sugar spikes and a 60% reduction in insulin levels after meals. Why? Because the Solus uses glucose directly from your bloodstream, not stored glycogen, meaning it burns sugar immediately. You can train at any time, sitting or standing. Simply lift your heels up and down slowly, pressing the front of your foot into the floor. If sitting, place a small weight or book on your knees for resistance. Do it for a few minutes after meals. It's simple, but incredibly effective. Reducing belly fat isn't about extreme diets or endless workouts. It's about understanding how your body works and working with it, not against it. By fixing your diet, improving sleep, managing stress, and moving daily, your metabolism starts to reset naturally. Take small steps, stay consistent, and the results will come faster than you think. If you found this video helpful, share it with someone who needs it. And I'll see you in the next episode.

Reverse Insulin Resistance With These 20 Doctor-Approved Secrets! | Senior Health tips: Kind: captions Language: en Your blood sugar looks normal, but inside your body, a silent storm may already be building. You feel tired after meals. Your belly fat won't go away. You crave sweets at night. Your blood pressure is creeping up. And your doctor says, "Just watch your sugar. But what if the real problem isn't just sugar? What if it's insulin resistance? The hidden condition behind type 2 diabetes, stubborn weight gain, fatty liver, heart disease, and even memory decline. Insulin resistance happens when your cells stop responding properly to insulin. Your pancreas works harder and harder, pumping out more insulin just to keep blood sugar stable. Over time, this leads to high insulin levels, inflammation, and metabolic damage. The good news, you can reverse it. And today I'm going to walk you through 20 powerful sciencebacked ways to reduce insulin resistance for good. First, walk after meals. Even a simple 10, 15inute walk right after eating helps your muscles absorb glucose without needing as much insulin. This alone can dramatically lower post-meal sugar spikes. Second, build muscle. Strength training two to three times per week improves insulin sensitivity because muscle tissue acts like a sponge for glucose. Third, reduce refined carbohydrates. White bread, sugary drinks, pastries, and processed snacks cause rapid spikes in blood sugar and insulin. Fourth, increase fiber intake. vegetables, beans, lentils, and chia seeds slow glucose absorption and improve gut health. Fifth, prioritize protein at every meal. Protein stabilizes blood sugar and reduces cravings. Sixth, sleep at least 7 hours per night. Poor sleep increases insulin resistance within days. Seventh, manage stress. Chronic stress raises cortisol which increases blood sugar and worsens insulin resistance. Deep breathing, prayer, meditation, or light stretching can help. Eighth, reduce belly fat gradually. Visceral fat releases inflammatory chemicals that worsen insulin resistance. Ninth, avoid sugary drinks completely. Liquid sugar is one of the fastest ways to spike insulin. 10th. Try timerestricted eating. Giving your body 12 to 14 hours without food allows insulin levels to drop and improves metabolic flexibility. 11th. Eat healthy fats. Olive oil, nuts, seeds, and fatty fish inflammation and improve insulin signaling. 12th. Stay hydrated. Dehydration can elevate blood sugar levels. 13th. Limit ultrarocessed foods. These foods combine sugar, refined oils, and additives that damage metabolic health. 14th. Increase magnesium intake. Magnesium supports insulin function and is often low in people with insulin resistance. 15th. Consider apple cider vinegar before meals. It may help reduce post-meal glucose spikes. 16th. Stop late night snacking. Eating late keeps insulin elevated while you sleep. 17th. Get morning sunlight. Natural light exposure helps regulate your circadian rhythm and improves metabolic health. 18th. Quit smoking. Smoking worsens insulin resistance and increases inflammation. 19th. Reduce seed oils and trans fats. These promote inflammation and metabolic dysfunction. 20th. Be consistent. Insulin resistance didn't develop overnight and it won't disappear overnight. Small daily habits compound into powerful results. Here's what most people don't understand. Insulin resistance is not just about diabetes. It is the root cause of many chronic diseases. High insulin levels can damage blood vessels, increase blood pressure, promote fat storage, and accelerate aging. But your body is incredibly adaptable. When you move more, eat whole foods, sleep well, and manage stress, your cells become responsive again. Your pancreas relaxes, inflammation decreases, energy improves, cravings fade. Within weeks, many people notice better energy and fewer crashes. Within months, waist size shrinks, blood tests improve, and the risk of type 2 diabetes drops dramatically. The key is not perfection. It's consistency. one walk, one healthy meal, one better night of sleep, then repeat. If you suspect insulin resistance, especially if you have belly fat, high triglycerides, low HDL, high fasting insulin, or a family history of diabetes, take action now. Don't wait for a diagnosis of diabetes to make changes. Your future health depends on what you do today. And the best time to reverse insulin resistance is before it turns into something far more serious. Start small. Stay consistent.

- Kenya & Éthiopie : les secrets des rois du Marathon.: Kind: captions Language: en How is this possible? How can two neighboring countries, where 30% of the population lives below the poverty line, dominate long-distance running for 50 years? Zigit in CFA who smashes the world record. Incredible first marathoner of all time under 2 hours, Kelvin Kipt, 23 years old, he was 2 hours old at the time I took this video. 46 of the 50 best marathon times are held by Kenyans or Ethiopians. And don't bother looking, it's the same for women. 43 of the best times are held by Kenyan or Ethiopian women. Simply put, no other athletics event is so dominated by one or two countries. So, obviously, it can't be the result of chance. Watching the World Championships or the Olympic Games, we've all wondered what they have that others don't. And everyone wants to provide an answer to this question. Some will say it's just a matter of genetics, others that it's the effects of living at altitude, their environment, and still others that the training method does everything and that it's a special training method that makes them stronger than others. The good news is that we now have enough information to look into the subject, understand the secret recipe that makes them so strong and perhaps even draw inspiration from it for our own little training as European amateur runners. You never know. The explanation we hear most often, and which on paper is the most logical, is that genetics makes them better. Kenyans and Ethiopians are born with the running gene. End of debate. Now, the real question is, does this running gene exist? So yes, it's obvious. The Kenyans and Ethiopians you see at the highest level, they are blessed by the gods. They have a knack for running. Otherwise, he simply wouldn't be able to compete at a high level. But if you see Americans, Europeans who are at the top level, well, it's the same. They have this gift too. A marathon runner who completes a marathon in 2 hours and 5 minutes, whether French, Spanish, Kenyan, Ethiopian, or American, has a gift, he has genetics that allow him to be at that level because running a marathon at more than 20 km/h is totally out of the ordinary. Now, the real question is, do they have an extra advantage that allows them to reach 2h3, 2h2, 2h1 and maybe one day less than 2h in the marathon? According to science, the verdict is that we don't really know. In any case, of the studies that have been done, most of them tell us that they found nothing. And I want to make it clear that they found nothing. That doesn't mean there aren't specificities, it means that for now we don't really have proof that they exist. The research will continue to progress. Science is often behind the curve because figuring out how the human body works is, well, not simple. You need to know where to look. If you do n't look in the right direction, you won't find it . Surprisingly, we find the same conclusion regarding VO2 max, which, as a reminder, is the maximum amount of oxygen your body is capable of consuming and, above all, using on the field. Yet, intuitively, one might think that being born at an altitude of over 2000 m because yes, the East African courses are on high plateaus between 2000 and 2800 m, but one might think that it is an advantage, that it allows one to develop one's body in a different way. And it's true, any teenager living on the high plateaus of East Africa has a VO2 max that would make almost 100% of Western adults envious. But at the very highest level, this difference is no longer so pronounced. Once you find yourself up against runners from all over the world who also have dream genetics, then everyone has an impressive VO2 max. And we even know that the runners who were measured with the highest VO2 max are not runners from East Africa. I am thinking in particular of the Norwegian endurance athletes who I believe had the highest VO2 max values. But if we look a little bit at what exists, well, corignion doesn't stand out, at least not on this indicator. Now it is true that VO2 max in itself is not the predominant indicator for long distances. It's important to have a big engine, yes, but it's mainly how you use it, how you use your body, that makes you efficient. That's why maybe if we look at the economics of racing, we'll learn some more interesting things. In 2023, a study reported that certain ethnic groups, such as the Kalengin in Kenya, from which many champions originate, may have a genetic mutation that could be of interest. Indeed, researchers claim that a mutation of the PIEZO1 gene is found in many runners from these tribes and results, among other things, in stiffer tendons. In practical terms, what does this change? Well, it's very simple. Tendons should be viewed as springs. We will store energy upon impact with the ground. energy that will be released at the moment of propulsion. So the stiffer our tendons are , the more energy they will be able to store and the more we will be able to release afterwards. It's free energy. We don't need to expend energy inside our bodies. We're just going to reuse energy as we go. So it's extremely economical and that's a huge advantage over long distances because the less energy you use to move forward, the more efficient you are and above all, the more energy you'll have left at the end of the race. Yes, that's a very schematic definition of the racing economy. I apologize in advance to physiology enthusiasts, but what you need to remember is that it is one of the main determinants of performance over long distances, at least in marathons, on roads where you will be repeating efforts and where conserving your energy is essential to last the distance. Well, in that respect, they would have a slight advantage. That doesn't mean that elsewhere in the world there aren't runners who have tendons as strong as the Kignans, but at least they do in proportion. That's something they have much more of. Moreover, beyond the gene aspect of genetics, we also see that the courorkignans or Ethiopians often have a morphology that is very adapted to long distances. In fact, you only need to look at them on a starting line. They are often very fine, slender, with very lean muscles, small but their tendons which will be long, thick will do a lot of the work. Again , this is not necessarily true among Ethiopians. We see a runner like Kenenissa Beleé who is much more muscular, shorter, stockier and well, he was an exceptional track runner. He was very, very good at marathons, but he wasn't the best marathon runner. Perhaps here we have a slight degree of difference. In any case, it proves that everything is minimal because despite his physique being less than optimal if we look at it in terms of marathon, well Beckelé, he still did 2h1. If this kind of topic interests you, let me know in the comments. Subscribe to the channel. I really need to know if this video test will be repeated in the future because I think it's interesting. I'm interested in it, but I primarily make the videos for you. So subscribe, it's the best indicator and the best way to support this channel. Returning to a more scientific perspective, Swedish physiologist B Salin, who has been very interested in the background current, estimated that on average, 400g less per calf translates into 8% energy savings with each stride. And at that point, I have to pause. The lesson to be learned from all this is certainly not that you have to look like a Kignan runner to run marathons. I'm going to go further . This certainly does not mean that you should not gain muscle mass in your calves. Firstly, because it is an observation made on the top 01% of runners. At our amateur level, there are an infinite number of levers to work on to progress and that deserve much more than dwelling on the shape and weight of our calves. But above all because you don't have the genetics of the Kignans. I remind you, they have tendons that are much more rigid and strong, and if you have calves that are too weak, well, you're going to get Achilles tendonitis, maybe plantar fasciitis. The calf muscle is a key muscle in performance. So the main thing for an amateur runner is to try to avoid injuries. Hey, it's not about trying to look like Kan and getting injured after 3 weeks. Anyway, I don't think there's a strategy for losing calf fat. In short, I insist because believing that changing our body will make it more efficient, well, firstly, it often generates serious eating disorders and secondly, it is absolutely not proven. We often come back to that. There are quite a few athletes who return to a higher weight than they had in previous years and who ultimately feel much better. So yes, it works for East African runners, but they are much more optimized for it. Naturally, they didn't try to achieve that. That's the big difference. That's it, end of the prevention break. Let's get back to our main topic. The second factor that explains the success of the East African courses is the geography of where they train. The high plateaus of Kenya's famous Rift Valley reach altitudes of between 1800 and 2400 m, creating what is known as a hypoxia phenomenon. Hypoxia is what happens when the body can no longer obtain enough oxygen to adequately supply its cells and tissues in general . Well, if we leave it at that , hypoxy seems to be the endurance runner's nightmare. We want maximum oxygen so we can run better. Well, that's without taking into account the extremely important adaptive capacities of the human body. In hypoxic conditions, the body increases the production of a hormone whose name may ring a bell. The PO. I'm sure that'll blow your mind . For you, PO is just a pan product. No, PO is a substance naturally produced by the human body. Doping is injecting EPO to simulate effects inside our body. But naturally, PO is just a hormone secreted by the body to stimulate the production of red blood cells . Because at altitude, you need more red blood cells to capture oxygen. When you know that it is these red blood cells that are responsible for transporting oxygen to our muscles, you know why European runners are keen to go and train in Kenya. It also works to melt in France or in any place that is located between 1500 and 2500 m. Roughly speaking, above that is too high because it will be difficult to train, and below that it has no effect. It is often said that an altitude training course only makes sense if you go for 1 to 3 weeks because that is the time it takes for our red blood cells to renew themselves. The difference with the Kignans is that they were born there, they have lived there 365 days a year their whole lives. Doesn't that have a slight advantage after a while? Doesn't the body simply adapt to that? Obviously, there is no doubt. Altitude and its adaptations are very clearly a disadvantage for East African runners. But there is always a "may" Ethiopia and Kenya are not the only regions in the world where people live between 1800 and 2500 m altitude. In fact, even in Europe, there are places like that. In Switzerland, in the Alps, in the Pyrenees, there are plenty of places where people live at those altitudes. And yet, we don't have the best runners in the world suddenly appearing out of nowhere . So that means that even if it is a factor, well we have to keep digging because it is not enough because there is another element on which we are perhaps not putting enough emphasis even though it is important, at least as important as genetics, I would say it is the place that running occupies in the country. Is running the number 1 sport in France? No, even though it's developing, it's going in the right direction. You don't generate vocations around running like you do in Kenya or Ethiopia where everyone runs, it's the sport. There is an even more important criterion, which is that in Kenya and Ethiopia, running is a way out of poverty. [Music] For most of those who become runners, agriculture is the number one means of subsistence in their region. So landing a professional contract with a sponsor, receiving a bonus on a race, these are opportunities that can literally change the life of a runner and his entire family. Just to give you an idea, by winning the Boston Marathon, Ethiopian Sisse l'EMA received a bonus of $150,000. It can be seen as high or not very high in relation to the investment and if we look at it in relation to other sports. But what's important is that this is equivalent to 62 times the average salary in Ethiopia. So obviously that motivates you to train even harder. In fact, if we're being factual, money is the number 1 factor that makes East African runners take up running. Perhaps not 20 or 30 years ago, but today it is . There is a study that asked the Kignans runners this question and this is what they answered. Don't put words in my mouth. That doesn't mean Kenyans don't like to run and that they only do it for money. This means that it is a very important motivating factor for them. The fact is that practicing a sport as a leisure activity or spending 100% of your time thinking about it and your recovery is not the same thing. In short, it's impossible to really know how much they love running and how much they do it for the money. What we do know is that in all the images we see from Kenya, when Europeans go there to film, they come across kids running next to them, smiling and looking like they're enjoying running. I know, it's not necessarily a happy reality to say that money is the number 1 criterion, but it is a reality and it is what can explain a factor that we put in the balance, in their success, doping. Because when you do it as a hobby or when you do it as something that can literally change your life from A to Z, going from absolute poverty to absolute wealth, well, that can explain certain behaviors. Personally, I don't want to go any further in this video about doping. I know the comments section will take care of it for me anyway. And yes, there are doping programs. Perhaps there is not enough control in those countries, that's true, but it's difficult to accuse without having evidence. What we do know is that there have been quite a few doping cases in recent years. In short, at this point in the video, I hope you understand that doping is not the major factor that makes the Kyans and Ethiopians better. Otherwise, we wouldn't have 46 of the 50 best pairs for marathon running made by these runners. I refuse to believe that these 46 riders are doped. Clearly, I don't believe it . Afterwards, in the water, it's certain that there are some , we can't deny it. Okay, if we go back to slightly more positive, cultural topics, well yes, running in Kenya is cultural. Today, for more than 50 years we have seen Kenyan and Ethiopian runners on international podiums bringing home medals and winning major races. So naturally, it permeates society. It's like here, when we see something that works, we want to get into the sport that has been publicized where we've seen French people succeed. It's a classic. And above all, I want to say that when you see that only Kanaks and Ethiopians win the big long-distance races, well, there's a positive effect in thinking, "Ah, we have an advantage, so I'll get involved too, I can do it too." It's difficult to know if it's a real effect or a placebo effect, but there's definitely a training effect and the more you believe in it, the more likely you are to succeed. The earlier you start, the earlier you believe in it, the more likely you are to succeed . It is often said that to achieve a great performance, you must first convince yourself that you have the potential. Well , clearly, the environment here is extremely conducive to performance, which has inspired a whole generation, then the next one, and so on for decades. Alright, let's move on to the part that many of you have been waiting for. the secret recipe for Kenyan-style training. Is there something that makes their training different from ours as Europeans and that makes them better? Well, in reality, there is no revolutionary session that the Ethiopians do that we do not. None. Perhaps that was the case 30 years ago, but today all the best European Americans go to train in Kenya. They see the sessions that the Kenans and the Ethiopians are holding. In short, they train together. So, there's no secret. For example, we talk about the famous Kenyan Fartlec. This famous session that is done once a week where it starts in a huge group, sometimes hundreds of runners, I'm sure you've already seen these images and where they alternate between fast minutes and slow minutes without really having speed, just by feeling. Well, actually, this kind of session has always existed. Sensational sessions, that's not something the Cyans invented. What makes a difference, however, is the density of runners, the emulation that can exist in groups with very high levels and little by little everyone improves thanks to others. But be careful, we also only see the tip of the iceberg. So we can see who will succeed because of this. But when you consider that there are several hundred runners in this famous session, will they all finish at an international level? No. There are also many who will either end up completely burned out and stop racing, or simply never reach the top level. If there are thousands and thousands of runners training like that in a super intense way like top athletes, well, it's so that in the end there are only a few hundred, a few dozen excellent ones and just a few who will end up on world and Olympic podiums. In short, if I go back to the method itself, does it really change anything from what we can do? No. two quality sessions per week, one long run and around that a lot of volume in basic endurance. In fact, it would be more of a mixture of several parameters which, taken together, would make training in these regions of the world so unique. And I think first and foremost of collective emulation. In Kenya, you can run with 50 runners who are around 2:05 marathoners. Does this happen elsewhere in the world? No, absolutely not. So naturally, when you train with all these runners, managing to achieve 2h4, 2h3, 2h2 is much easier because the collective emulation leads everyone to progress. Whereas if you do it all alone in your corner, well, it's you and yourself who have to succeed in self-motivating. There is another point that I find fundamental, which is the almost monastic life that these runners demonstrate. Run, eat, rest. That's all there is in their lives. 7 days a week , 365 days a year. And that, well, that's one of the keys to success. Proper recovery during training is essential for good progress and repetition over the long or even very long term. There are no other secrets in reality and it works for everyone, not just for the Kignans. The more you want to succeed in improving at something, the more you have to practice it and the more you have to do what is necessary on the side to recover well, to integrate well. Because here I'm talking about running, but if you want to learn the piano, it's the same thing. You will repeat this every day. You will make sure you sleep well to properly integrate what you have learned, and so on day by day, month by month, year by year. It sounds crazy, but in fact, the simplicity of their lives is probably their best-kept secret. The simpler life is, the more it is oriented around an activity to succeed in it, and the more effective it will be . And I'm sure that international runners, with all the distractions we have in our modern lives, have less of that ability to be 100% focused on their activity. Afterwards, far be it from me to criticize our Western lives, you know, firstly because we are not elite runners so we are not really concerned, but Western lives are more stressful, richer in distractions, every detail counts and I think that this one is much more than a detail in reality. Another point not to be overlooked, Iten a ditadba, it's not Le Havre. There is a wide variety of terrain, from flat to hilly, and including paths. In short, it has everything you need to develop a harmonious body. And to finish on the training philosophy, I would like to return to a point which for me is very important. The relationship that the Kignans have with their sensations, the stopwatch, speeds, that's not their thing. That's why they like Fartleake. That's why in races, they will also run with variations in pace. It's not like us where everything is timed to the millimeter, we respect a certain pace. For them, it's really about feeling. They feel it inside, they know what to do. Today, we are so used to our watch, our pace, having our heart rate, in short, being able to see everything in real time, that if we leave the watch at home, we will be totally lost. The majority of Kenyan or Ethiopian runners don't have all of that, especially at the beginning of their careers. They are forced to go by feel or with a stopwatch when they are going around a track. But even that, the track, is not something that is common. They will mainly train on trails in fartlec, in groups, trying to gauge themselves and in fact that's how you learn these sensations and can use them better afterwards. A little anecdote about that, I can tell you about it because when I started running, well there was no GPS, there was no heart rate monitor, so I had to train by feel. And today, I know that I have a body knowledge reference system that is extremely useful to use. I know how to run at different intensities and I am able to find my paces to within 5 seconds per kilometer. But to be able to master it, well, you have to test it. So during training, don't hesitate to not rely solely on your watch. Sometimes, just go with the flow a little and at the end of the interval ask yourself, "Did I manage to do what I planned?" Yes, no, and I'm calibrating myself a little bit. This will definitely be useful for your shopping. The parenthesis has ended at a very high level. Yes, the Cyans and the Ethiopians, I use watches. They try to stay in sync with the pace, but they learned their feel for it before that. It's ingrained within me, and I'm sure that's an advantage. By the way, have you ever tried going for a run without a watch? Let me know in the comments what the result was . I'm interested in reading that. Training makes you regress, it's recovery that makes you progress. You've been hearing me say that phrase for 10 years now, and let me tell you, the best in the world to apply it are the runners from East Africa. We saw it in their monastic lifestyle and well, in fact we're going to go into a little more detail about how they eat, for example. It's super interesting because in a world where we're told about the ketogenic diet, about consuming a lot of protein, in short, there's a little bit of everything being said in the modern world, well, if we look at the diet of the Caignans for their endurance, well, in fact they consume a lot of complex carbohydrates, mostly with little fat and not that much protein. I'm not saying that's what you should eat every day, but that's what they do and it works for them. In short, nothing drastically transformed there. I imagine you doubt that natural foods can be transformed and that they have many of the nutrients we need to develop. And by the way, that also means that eating healthily doesn't mean eating vegetables, vegetables, vegetables. That means a lot of calories. Because to run 200 kilometers a week, let me tell you, they burn a lot of calories, otherwise they wouldn't make it to the end of the week. Now, once again, we are not Kignan runners. That doesn't mean we can't enjoy ourselves . We face quite a few temptations on a daily basis. They don't necessarily have them, but there are other things that don't consume it. Alcohol, tobacco, all that stuff, it's zero. You don't find that in a training center in Kenya. Simply put, after a hard workout or a long run, a nice cup of tea and massage or a nap is more appropriate. In fact, that's where it gets interesting because what we've been looking for from the beginning is what Ethiopian colors could have that's different, what extra something is needed. Well, in this case, it's more like what they're doing less of. The simpler life is, the more beneficial it seems to be for progress. In concrete terms, we see no extreme recovery protocol, no specific object that allows them to recover. They'll just sit in a chair and do nothing and sleep a lot. In short, this proves to us once again that the trio of sleep, nutrition, hydration and I would say stress management, now I'm adding this one more and more, a calm life where we manage all these aspects well, well, that's apparently the key to success. Among the obvious advantages, yes, there is a natural talent among the Kignans and the Ethiopians. We've seen small details in their genetics that seem to give them an advantage, but the mass effect, especially the fact that they're all into running, that they all try it very early, that they train a lot, well, that's surely what leads them to have many runners at the top of the pack. To draw an analogy with another sport that is dear to my heart, genetics is a bit like the driving talent of an F1 driver. It's important, but without a high-performing and well-tuned car, it's impossible to win the race. Finally, that's without taking Max Verstappen into account because he's proving me wrong right now . But anyway, that's another topic, we're not going to talk about F1 here. Maybe one day there will be a channel dedicated to F1. Who knows? The second lesson is that we may be placing too much importance on the training method. Running communities love to talk about the Norwegian method, the Kenane method, the double threshold and blah blah blah and blah blah blah . The reality is that having a method, being regular, sticking to it, and recovering well surely accounts for 90% of the work. African marathon runners have n't reinvented the wheel, and we can see that the fundamentals of their training are very similar to what we can offer you on campus, quite simply. And the last piece of information I take away from this video is that the modern lifestyle may have an impact on the quality of the colors that will emerge. A child who grows up in rural Kenya and has to walk several kilometers every day develops a great deal of adaptation that a sedentary urban child will not develop. Even if he goes running at athletics school several times a week, it's not enough. And I'm not saying that to romanticize life in Kenya or Ethiopia. In practical terms, it works for running, but overall it is harder than in our countries. But we must realize that the time we spend sitting in front of a TV, a console or any other connected device, the quality of what we eat, how we move in general, well, that will shape us for the future. And so all of this has an impact on the performance of today's youth, who are the future elite. Now, to go into more detail about the training, I'm sharing the video where I detailed Luc Kip Shoget's training , the one that allowed him to achieve his stratospheric marathon times and, above all, to last over time because he's been at a high level for 20 years, 22 years even. His first race was in 2003 when he became world champion in the 5000m. That's crazy. Alright, I'm saying goodbye. Until next time, friends. Yeah.

- How Does Biomarker Tracking Boost Longevity And Wellness? - Long Life Blueprint: Kind: captions Language: en How does biomarker tracking boost longevity and wellness? Imagine having a personal health coach that gives you real-time feedback on how your body is doing. That's what biioarker tracking can do for you. It provides measurable biological indicators that reflected your health and aging process. This means you can see how your lifestyle choices impact your well-being over time. Biomarker tracking focuses on specific biological markers. These include blood glucose levels, cholesterol profiles, inflammation markers, and even estimates of biological age. By regularly measuring these markers, you can get a clear picture of your health. This helps you understand how your body is responding to things like diet, exercise, and stress. One of the main benefits of biomarker tracking is early detection and prevention. By spotting changes in your biomarkers before any symptoms appear, you can take action. This proactive approach can help you manage risks for diseases like heart disease and diabetes. Instead of waiting for health issues to arise, you can focus on prevention. Another advantage is personalized health insights. With tools like continuous glucose monitors, you can see how your daily habits affect your health in real time. This allows you to make tailored adjustments to your nutrition and physical activity. Knowing how your body reacts helps you optimize your health outcomes. Biomarker tracking also helps you understand your biological age. Unlike chronological age, biological age reflects how well your body systems are functioning. By monitoring this, you can assess the impact of lifestyle changes aimed at improving your health span, which is the period of life spent in good health. Additionally, biomarkers guide interventions for longevity. They serve as measurable endpoints to evaluate the effectiveness of therapies or lifestyle changes. This datadriven approach supports informed decision-making about your health. Maintaining better health through biomarker tracking can also support your financial security. By reducing the risk of chronic diseases, you can lower health care costs. This contributes to greater financial stability as you age, allowing you to preserve your independence. Combining biomarker data with technology and coaching can further boost your motivation. Wearable devices and personalized coaching help you stick to healthier behaviors. Increased physical activity and better lifestyle choices lead to improved longevity and wellness. In practical terms, biomarker tracking involves regular testing of key indicators. This includes fasting blood glucose, long-term glucose control measures, cholesterol levels, inflammation markers, and complete blood counts. Advances in technology have improved the accuracy of biological age predictions, making it easier to monitor Overall, biomarker tracking empowers you to make informed decisions about your health. It aligns with a preventative care approach by focusing on maintaining function and preventing disease. This not only promotes wellness but also helps secure financial resources by reducing the burden of age- related health conditions. [Music]

We're&nbsp; a spin-off from Stanford University, building&nbsp;&nbsp; upon 17 years of research for my co-founders,&nbsp; professors Tomo and Joe D. Simone.
