Our first target market&nbsp; is in the management of heart failure patients. By&nbsp;&nbsp; 2030, 8 million Americans will suffer from heart&nbsp; failure, costing our system about $70 billion&nbsp;&nbsp; annually. When these patients are discharged from&nbsp; the hospital, often times they get lost. Nearly&nbsp;&nbsp; one in four patients return back to the hospital&nbsp; within 30 days. This is completely unacceptable.&nbsp;&nbsp; The standard of care today is for a patient&nbsp; to weigh himself or herself on a daily basis.&nbsp;&nbsp; Unfortunately, this is a non-specific and very&nbsp; much a lagging indicator of acute decompensation.&nbsp;&nbsp; This is a big problem. At Adaptics, we're building&nbsp; a continuous sensor that measures five biomarkers&nbsp;&nbsp; continuously. The electrolytes, potassium and&nbsp; sodium, kidney function markers, creatine and ura,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the gold standard cardiac biomarker, NT&nbsp; Probnp. We&nbsp; know the faster we get these patients on the&nbsp;&nbsp; highest level of medications, the better their&nbsp; outcomes. Secondly, longitudinally tracking&nbsp;&nbsp; this data allows physicians to understand and&nbsp; see decompensation happen days to even weeks&nbsp;&nbsp; before it can devolve into acute decompensation.&nbsp; Finally, at about the cost of a CGM, we can offer&nbsp;&nbsp; this technology to almost anyone who needs it.&nbsp; The readmission opportunity alone is about 12&nbsp;&nbsp; billion. And in addition to that, we have a market&nbsp; for medicine titration and long-term monitoring.&nbsp;&nbsp; Our technology is based on a break breakthrough&nbsp; chemistry at Stanford that allows us to create&nbsp;&nbsp; synthetic DNA molecular switches that can bind&nbsp; to nearly any molecule that we design for. This&nbsp;&nbsp; is a much more flexible platform compared to a&nbsp; CGM which is limited to just a few molecules.&nbsp;&nbsp; We've demonstrated this technology in vitro&nbsp; over about 12 analytes and over the last two&nbsp;&nbsp; and a half quarters we've begun our first&nbsp; in human studies beyond heart failure. - What Is Biomarker Tracking For Longevity? - Long Life Blueprint: Kind: captions Language: en What is biomarker tracking for longevity? Imagine having a window into your body's aging process that updates regularly. That's what biomarker tracking offers. It's like a health dashboard that shows how well your body is functioning at a cellular level. Instead of waiting for symptoms to appear, you get early clues about potential health issues. This way, you can make adjustments before problems become serious. Biomarker tracking involves measuring specific biological signs that reflected how your body ages and how healthy you are overall. These signs include blood sugar levels, cholesterol, and fats in your blood. They also include markers of inflammation like C reactive protein protein. These help show if your body is inflamed or at risk of heart disease. Another important marker is telomeir length. Telomeirs are protective caps on your chromosomes that shorten as you age. Shorter telomeirs are linked to faster aging. Biomarker tracking also uses biological age estimates based on DNA. These estimates look at patterns of DNA methylation which change as you get older. This gives a more accurate picture of your true aging process compared to just counting years. To track these markers, you might do blood tests, use wearable devices, or get advanced lab analyses. For example, continuous glucose monitors can track your blood sugar levels throughout the day, helping you see how your diet and activity affect your health. Regularly checking these markers helps you spot early signs of health decline. It supports preventative care so you can take steps to improve your health before issues develop. This might mean changing your diet, increasing exercise, or adjusting sleep habits. Tracking biomarkers also helps you see how your lifestyle choices impact your aging process over time. In terms of longevity and wellness, this datadriven approach can save you money by preventing chronic diseases that are costly to treat later. It motivates healthier behaviors, which can lead to better quality of life and reduced health care expenses. By keeping an eye on your biological markers, you're actively managing your health and aging process. This proactive approach shifts focus from waiting for illness to preventing it, helping you stay vital longer. In short, biomarker tracking is a way to understand and influence how your body ages. It provides personalized insights that guide your choices for a longer, healthier life. It's about using science to stay ahead of aging and maintain your well-being for years to come. [Music]

First goal is to demonstrate the potential of digital biomarkers to advance personalized health whether for personal use or for implementation in your clinical trial or clinical practice. The other goal is to share with you my enthusiasm about R to collect analyze and in particular visualize digital health data. With digital biomarkers I refer to objective and quantifiable data collected by digital devices such as apps sensors and wearables that provide information about our physiology behavior or environment. Typically such digital devices allow continuous if not at least more frequent collection of data and thereby instead of comparing a single time point to a population mean enable more individualized feedback even in real time. A perfect example of molecular biomarkers gone digital are the recent advances in blood glucose monitoring. Various sensor-based devices now exist that allow continuous or flash measurement of blood sugar levels. This helps patients in managing their diabetes but can also provide insights in individuals response to different food types. Another digital biomarker that deserves specific mention is heart rate variability. Heart rate variability or HRV is the variation in the time between consecutive heartbeats. This variation in heart rhythm is a good thing. It keeps the heart flexible so it can easily adapt to sudden changes or stressors. When we are under stress the variability in our heart typically lowers. HRV is relatively easy to measure with commercially available heart rate monitors. I use HRV myself as an aid to better listen to my body by taking 3-minute short-term HRV measurement every morning at my awakening using a heart rate monitor on my finger in combination with the HRV for training app. In this chart you can see my HRV fluctuating over time indicating a period of stress about here in the middle of May as well as a strong response of my body to my second COVID 19 vaccination indicated by a strong drop in the HRV. In a similar way as HRV I track my long-term trends for various other parameters. For most of these I don't really care about the numbers at the moment. I just want to keep track and build my personal database. But that's not always easy. At the moment I first analyzed my step count I tracked with the Apple Watch which was about here in uh after a year of wearing the Apple Watch in October last year I was shocked by my sedentary lifestyle. As you see I hardly reached the 5,000 steps a day. From that moment on I started to almost obsessively track my step counts to make sure I did hit at least the 10,000 steps a day as you can see later on. In the same way actually in the name of science I measured my body weight every single day using the Cardio Scale. Although I tried hard to not to look at the scale every day I can't help to do so and then feel a little bit frustrated now and then but I really like to keep on collecting this data and just see how my weight evolves over time. Probably the easiest thing to track is your sleep. You don't have to do anything for it. Just sleep and your data is ready when you wake up. Therefore I'm using the Dream headband that measures brain activity and besides tracking the amount of sleep motion and heart rate the product algorithm can make a pretty accurate prediction of my sleep phases. So I use the sleep phases to gain more insights in my sleep. I have for example plotted the frequency of my sleep phases along the night. And here you can see that sleeping in is not likely to get me more deep sleep deep sleep which is needed for recovery as deep sleep is this purple band it occurs mostly in the beginning of the night. So for increasing my amount of deep sleep I'd better make sure to go to bed in time. In addition I keep track of my nightly patterns in HRV which should show an increase during the night as an indication as a sign of recovery as well as my heart rate also measured with the Oura ring which should show an early decrease as a sign of recovery during the deep sleep. Another interesting thing about sleep or lifestyle in general is regularity. Going to bed and waking up at about the same time each night is important to keep our bodies biological clocks functioning correctly and not to suffer from a so-called social jet lag. As you can see during the 1 and 1/2 year of the pandemic my sleep pattern has been nearly excellent. No late night parties on the weekend no needs to sleep in and no early morning flights to catch. My eating patterns on the other hand is a different story. Working from home has especially messed up my lunch time. On some days I'd have my lunch already at 11:00 a.m. whereas on other days I'd completely forget about lunch until late in the afternoon. Talking about working from home and for myself I got some other interesting insights using the RescueTime app on my laptop and mobile devices. As you see I start to use my mobile phone almost first thing in the morning and yes I also seem to go to bed with it. Um you can also see that I have a hard time to close my laptop in the evening or put aside my work in the weekends. So it's clear that I got hooked on data science and data visualization. It's actually pretty addictive. And with so much data that I'm collecting there's always something more to visualize to analyze um another R blog post to read or I must admit another coding issue to be solved. As another data visualization example I'd like to end my talk with this data visualization of patterns around my monthly period. In this chart where each ring represents another cycle you see how the Oura ring picked up the rising body temperature after ovulation. Although not very surprising I was more surprised to see a similar pattern for my breathing rate during sleep. Apparently some hormonal influences caused my breathing rate to be higher at the end and the beginning of each new cycle. And the same hormonal influences could be seen for the days that I noted a headache. Although I still don't know what is triggering the headaches it is very obvious from this plot that my headaches tend to occur only during the first half of my menstrual cycle. And with that observation I'd like to end my talk and I hope you agree with me that you are what you measure. And if I am what I measure then well I sleep I move I work and I use R a lot for about 2 hour almost 2 hours a day every day including the weekends and holidays over the past year. But I'm excited to keep on working. I look forward to update and update my skills further to automate and share the data upload analysis and visualization tools that I have worked on so far. I hope to create an interactive shiny dashboard but most of all I really look forward to working together on some awesome digital health and data vis projects. So please get in touch if you have any ideas on those projects. I'd be happy to hear from you. For now I'd like to thank you for listening and again please get in touch. I'd love to hear from you. Bye.

- How Does Biomarker Tracking Identify Personal 'super-responders'? - Long Life Blueprint: Kind: captions Language: en How does biomarker tracking identify personal super responders? Imagine discovering that your body reacts to certain health strategies in a way that others might not. That's what biomarker tracking helps us do. It looks at specific molecules, genes, or proteins in your body to see how you respond to treatments or lifestyle changes. Some people called super responders show extraordinary and lasting improvements for beyond what most experience. Biomarkers are measurable signs that tell us about your health. They can include genetic markers, blood based proteins, metabolic profiles or immune system signals. By analyzing these, we can understand how your body reacts to different interventions. The process starts with a baseline assessment. Before any treatment begins, you get tested for various biomarkers. This might include genetic sequencing, blood tests for inflammation, hormone levels, or metabolic indicators. These tests create a detailed picture of your unique biological makeup. Next, your biioarkers are monitored regularly during and after the intervention. For example, if you start a new exercise routine or take supplements, your biomarkers are checked periodically. Super respponders often show clear signs like big drops in inflammation markers, better metabolic function, or increased cellular repair signals. These changes are more pronounced than in typical responders. Scientists then compare data from super responders with regular responders. They look for patterns in the biomarkers that are linked to exceptional results. Certain genetic traits or molecular signals might stand out as indicators of who is likely to be a super responder. Using this information, predictive models are built. These models help forecast who will respond strongly to specific treatments or lifestyle changes. This means you can get a personalized plan that's more likely to work well for you. In practical terms, biomarker tracking can reveal who benefits most from anti-aging protocols, nutritional plans, or exercise routines. For example, some people might see dramatic improvements in inflammation or metabolic health, showing they are super respponders to those interventions. This approach makes health care more efficient. It helps target treatments to those who will benefit most, saving time and resources. It also allows individuals to tailor their wellness strategies based on their biological responses rather than relying on general advice. In the end, biomarker tracking pinpoints why some people experience exceptional benefits from certain therapies. It uncovers their unique biological signatures, guiding personalized age management and wellness plans. This way, you can focus on what works best for your body, helping you stay healthier and more vibrant for longer. [Music]

I would much rather see that glucose at 73 and that triglyceride at 50 which to me would say, oh, this cell's processing through energy great, and things are moving through and we're not backing up in the bloodstream, we're not converting to triglycerides. So that's where really optimal ranges get in. But so that's glucose and triglycerides why if those are starting to creep up it's a sign that something is happening metabolically. And then when we look at some of the other biomarkers, so hemoglobin A1C is really, so that's a marker that's looking at how many of the hemoglobin molecules that are in the red blood cells that carry oxygen, how many of those hemoglobin molecules have sugar stuck to them? And that's glycation. So you're looking at glycated hemoglobin and you can imagine that if the concentration of glucose is higher over time, more glucose is going to stick to red blood cells and that's going to create a higher percentage of glycated hemoglobin. So that's why that lab is represented as a percentage. So less than 5.7 is what we want. ANDREW HUBERMAN: You want those cells nice and smooth and slippery. CASEY MEANS: Nice and smooth. No sugar stuck to them causing dysfunction. And because blood cells last for about 90 to 120 days, hemoglobin A1C is giving us a basically a snapshot of average blood sugar levels over 90 to 120 days. And if that average is higher, again, probably a sign that cells are rejecting glucose from the cell and it's causing a rise in And then just talking about one other biomarker in that, that we talked about which was blood pressure. People might say, well, how does blood pressure relate to like what's going on inside the cell in the mitochondria and whatnot? And really the fascinating link is that when that cell becomes insulin resistance which again is a compensatory mechanism for mitochondrial dysfunction, the insulin is going to rise in the blood because the body's insulin resistance. So the body's going to churn out more insulin to try and overcome the insulin block to drive the sugar into the cells. So insulin levels rise. While insulin is one of the key activators of nitric oxide which is the molecule in the blood that dilates and relaxes blood vessels. And so when we become insulin resistant and we're not responding to that insulin signal, we end up getting less nitric oxide activity. So this is how looking at even these very basic, very cheap biomarkers through the lens of basic cellular physiology, we can start to see, man, my body might be like under-powered. I might have a mitochondrial issue here. So that's a few of those tests that we really want to shoot for. ANDREW HUBERMAN: Thank you for tuning in to the Huberman Lab Clips channel. If you enjoyed the clip that you just viewed, please check out the full length episode by clicking here.

- How Can You Simplify Time-consuming Daily Biomarker Tracking? - Long Life Blueprint: Kind: captions Language: en How can you simplify time-consuming daily biomarker tracking? Imagine being able to keep tabs on your health without spending hours each day. That's the goal of making biomarker tracking easier and more efficient. Instead of manual logs or frequent lab visits, modern technology offers smart solutions. Using wearable devices like fitness trackers or smartwatches can automatically record data such as heart rate, sleep quality, and physical activity. These gadgets sync with apps that store your information securely, saving you time and effort. Some devices also measure biochemical markers through non-invasive sensors which can monitor things like glucose or cortisol levels continuously. This means you don't have to prick your finger or visit a clinic daily. Another way to simplify is by integrating sensors that provide real-time data. These bio sensors can be worn or embedded in your environment and track specific molecules without needing frequent blood draws. For example, some sensors can detect stress hormones or blood sugar levels with minimal effort. The key is choosing devices that connect seamlessly with your smartphone or computer, so data flows automatically into your health dashboard. This reduces manual input and helps you see trends over time without extra work. Automating data management is also essential. Using platforms that analyze your biomarker data can identify patterns and alert you to potential issues. These systems often use machine learning to personalize insights based on your history. For instance, if your cortisol levels rise consistently at certain times, the system can suggest lifestyle changes. This way, you get meaningful feedback without having to interpret complex charts yourself. Focusing on a small set of important biioarkers makes tracking more manageable. Instead of trying to monitor dozens of markers daily, select those most relevant to your health goals. For longevity and age management, common markers include blood sugar, cholesterol, inflammation levels, and hormone balance. Prioritizing these reduces the time and cost involved while still giving you valuable health information. Home testing kits are another helpful tool. These kits allow you to collect blood or saliva samples at home and send them digitally for analysis. Results are then uploaded to your health app, making it easy to review progress without clinic visits. Many kits now cost between $50 and $100 and provide accurate results within a few days. Using labelf-free bio sensors and minimal sample preparation techniques can speed things up even more. These advanced methods analyze biomarkers quickly and cost effectively, often in minutes, without needing complex lab work. This saves both time and money, making daily tracking more practical. Finally, setting up automated alerts and reminders can keep you on track. Apps can notify you when it's time to collect a sample or calibrate your devices. They can also alert you if a biioarker reading falls outside healthy ranges, prompting quick action. This way, you stay consistent without constantly thinking about tracking. Combining wearable tech, continuous bio sensors, automated data analysis, and focused biomarker choices creates a simple, effective system. It supports your wellness journey and helps you manage aging proactively all while saving time and reducing hassle. [Music]

- Mobile Biomarkers : How Wearables Detect Arrhythmias and aFib Early: Kind: captions Language: en Today's medical ccentric topic is mobile biomarkers. How wearables detect arhythmias and aphib early. Why wearables are changing heart rhythm detection. Heart rhythm disorders like atrial fibrillation aphib are often silent and intermittent making them easy to miss during clinic visits. Many patients have no symptoms until a stroke or heart failure occurs. Wearable devices are changing this by enabling continuous real world heart rhythm monitoring outside the hospital. What mobile biomarkers actually are. Mobile biomarkers are physiological signals collected continuously by wearing devices such as smart watches, rings or chest patches. These include heart rate, heart rhythm variability, pulse wave patterns and activity linked changes. When analyzed over time, these signals can reveal early signs of arhythmias. How wearables detect heart rhythm abnormalities. Most consumer wearables use photoplenosis monography, PPG, a light-based sensor that measures changes in blood flow with each heartbeat. Irregular pulse patterns detected by PPG can suggest arrhythmias. Some devices also include single lead ECG, allowing users to record a rhythm strip when symptoms occur. Detecting atrial fibrillation early. Aphib is characterized by an irregularly irregular rhythm without a consistent pattern. Wearables use algorithms to identify irregular pulse intervals over prolonged periods. Repeated irregularity, especially at rest or during sleep, raises suspicion for AIB, even when the user feels well. Why continuous monitoring matters? Traditional ECGs capture only a few seconds of rhythm. Aphib can be peroximal, occurring sporadically and resolving spontaneously. Wearables monitor for days to months, dramatically increasing the chance of detecting brief or infrequent episodes that would otherwise go unnoticed. Accuracy and validation. Large studies have shown that wearable-based AIB detection has high sensitivity for screening. False positives can occur due to motion artifacts, premature beats, or poor sensor contact. For this reason, wearable alerts are considered screening tools, not diagnostic confirmation. What happens after an alert? When a wearable flags possible aphib or arhythmia, confirmation with medical grade ECG monitoring is required. This may include halter monitors, patch monitors, or event recorders. Early detection allows timely initiation of stroke prevention and rhythm or rate control strategies. Beyond AIB, other arrhythmias wearables may also detect breda cardia, tacicardia, pauses, and frequent premature beats. Trends in resting heart rate and heart rate variability can provide early clues to autonomic imbalances and evolving cardiac disease. Limitations to understand. Wearables are less reliable during high movement, may miss very brief episodes, and cannot classify complex arrhythmias accurately. Data interpretation requires clinical context. Why this matters for prevention? Early AIB detection enables stroke risk assessment and anti-coagulation before catastrophic events occur. Wearables shift care from reactive to preventive cardiology. Thank you for watching our video. Please do not forget to like and share the video. Also, please subscribe to the channel to stay updated on our latest videos.

resource compendium and the supplemental practice aids for clinicians afterwards hello miss elkins my name is rashida persinger and i am the nurse practitioner who partners with your doctor it is my understanding you have further questions surrounding biomarker testing and why it is needed before proceeding with treatment is this correct yes i feel overwhelmed with my diagnosis of advanced lung cancer and all the new terminology i need to understand could you explain again why i need biomarker testing i read something online about testing for mutations is that the same as biomarker testing great questions biomarkers are measurable characteristics of your cells that can provide information about your lung cancer certain biomarkers are drivers such as mutations that cause cancer to grow and spread mutation testing or molecular testing or biomarker testing are all terms that refer to the same thing in case of targeted therapies biomarker looks at the genetic makeup of the cancer or its dna this is different from the dna we are born with this dna makeup can provide information to guide proper treatment options for your lung cancer as clinicians i think my takeaway points for other clinicians is to understand the role that we play when patients present with this new diagnosis with new information being thrown at them and the expectation to understand everything in one sit down we have to avail ourselves to be able to break down terminology be available multiple times to explain these terms so that for when patients walk out from us or when they are talking with their family members or when they are trying to communicate and understand it for their selves it is in their terms under their way and so therefore they can have a good knowledge base of why we need to do biomarker testing and why it's important to have all the results and why we would like to wait and hold off for chemotherapy in my experience i have found that when we take that time to explain to patients sometimes multiple times that anxiety level decreases and they have a better understanding of why it is so important in having these results i think one of the most difficult things for patients when they get a diagnosis of advanced lung cancer is understanding all of the new terminology that all of a sudden is being thrown at them there's just so many new terms that unless you already have a background in something medical that as a brand new patient it's hard to understand biomarker testing targeted therapy etc and even if it's explained to you sometimes it takes several explanations to really be able to comprehend everything so once we've done our proper testing and we find what we're looking for which is an actionable driver what next how do we select our initial
